coredata.dc:title: A comprehensive model of carbon nanodots with 0.21 nm lattice fringes patterns
coredata.dc:creator: Boukhvalov, Danil W.
Osipov, Vladimir Yu.
Murzalinov, Danatbek
Serikkanov, Abay
Bi, Hong
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we design the model for carbon nanodots with 0.21 nm regular lattice fringes patterns in a TEM image observed in multiple experiments. Based on the structural and microscopic data analysis, we proposed a system built from AA-stacked graphene layers with several interlayer covalent C–C bonds and functional groups on the surface. Evaluation of the stability of considered systems upon change in the number of constituting graphene layers, breaking interlayer covalent C–C bonds, formation of vacancies, and changes of functional groups on the surface have been carried out. We also demonstrate the dependence of the bandgap of such carbon nanodots upon the described conditions. The possible assembly mechanism of such a system was also discussed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Transmission electron microscopy
Lattice fringes
Density functional theory
Carbon nanodots
Luminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Some basic principles of graphene chemistry
Models with a minimal number of interlayer covalent bonds
Model with a larger number of interlayer covalent bonds
Simulation of the formation of AA-stacked graphenic systems

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Danil W. Boukhvalov reports financial support was provided by Committee of Science of the Ministry of Science and Higher Education of the Republic of Kazakhstan. Danatbek Murzalinov reports financial support was provided by Committee of Science of the Ministry of Science and Higher Education of the Republic of Kazakhstan. Abay Serikkanov reports financial support was provided by Committee of Science of the Ministry of Science and Higher Education of the Republic of Kazakhstan. Vladimir Yu. Osipov reports financial support was provided by Physical-Technical Institute named after A F Ioffe Russian Academy of Sciences. Hong Bi reports financial support was provided by National Natural Science Foundation of China. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.D.W.B. D.M. and A.S. acknowledge the support of the Science Committee of the Ministry of Science and Higher Education of the Republic of Kazakhstan (Grant No. BR21881954). The research conducted by V.Yu.O. was carried out within the framework of the Ioffe Institute project FFUG-2024-0019. H. B. acknowledges the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52172033).

D.W.B., D.M., and A.S. acknowledge the support of the Science Committee of the Ministry of Science and Higher Education of the Republic of Kazakhstan (Grant No. BR21881954 ). The research conducted by V.Yu.O. was carried out within the framework of the Ioffe Institute project FFUG-2024-0019. H. B. acknowledges the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52172033 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM (a,b) and AFM (c) images of CNDs with TEM lattice fringes and top and side views of bilayer graphene with AA-stacking (d). The details of synthesis and characterization of the nanoparticles shown in panels (a) and (b) are reported in Refs. [31,32].

Optimized atomic structure of the unit (a) and supercell (b) used for the simulation of graphene bilayer with AA-stacking and ratio of carbon atoms in sp
3 and sp
2 hybridization 1:2 (a). Unit cells used for simulation of graphene bilayer AA-stacking and with ratio of carbon atoms in sp
3 and sp
2 hybridization 1:4 (c), graphene trilayer with AA-stacking and ratio of carbon atoms in sp
3 and sp
2 hybridization 1:4 (d). Panel (e) shows the electronic structure of the systems shown in panels (a) and (c).

Optimized atomic structure of supercells used for the simulation of graphene bilayer with AA-stacking and ratio of carbon atoms in sp
3 and sp
2 hybridization 1:1 (a), bilayer with ratio of carbon atoms in sp
3 and sp
2 hybridization 3:4 bi- (b), tri- (c) and four-layers (d,e) of graphene with AA-stacking and ratio of carbon atoms in sp
3 and sp
2 hybridization 1:2. Density-of-states functions of the carbon structures presented in panels (a), (c) and (d,e) are shown in panel (f). Panel (g) shows the density of states functions of the two-layered structures presented in panels (a) and (b).

Optimized atomic structure of the systems shown in Fig. 3d and e after the formation of double vacancy in the internal graphene layer (a), and removed one carbon bridge with the simultaneous formation in two monovacancies in different internal layers (b) and corresponding densities of states. Optimized atomic structure of the systems shown in Fig. 3d and e after substituting the half of hydroxyl groups on the surfaces by –CH2CH3 groups (d) or in the liquid media (e), and corresponding densities of states.

Optimized atomic structures of two nano-graphenes connected by covalent bridge on the edge (a), graphene oxide after covalent attaching benzoic acid (b,d), one of the products of the interactions of two molecules of citric acid (d), seed of nano-graphene obtained at initial stages of CNDs synthesis from citric acid (e), and possible product of the interaction three molecules of OPD with two molecules of acetic acid (f).

head.ce:title: A comprehensive model of carbon nanodots with 0.21 nm lattice fringes patterns

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Materials Physics and Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we design the model for carbon nanodots with 0.21 nm regular lattice fringes patterns in a TEM image observed in multiple experiments. Based on the structural and microscopic data analysis, we proposed a system built from AA-stacked graphene layers with several interlayer covalent C–C bonds and functional groups on the surface. Evaluation of the stability of considered systems upon change in the number of constituting graphene layers, breaking interlayer covalent C–C bonds, formation of vacancies, and changes of functional groups on the surface have been carried out. We also demonstrate the dependence of the bandgap of such carbon nanodots upon the described conditions. The possible assembly mechanism of such a system was also discussed.

ce:section.ce:para: More than thirty thousand scientific papers about carbon nanodots (CNDs) [1–5] have been published since the first report in 2004 [6]. Since not all reported CNDs demonstrate quantum dots-related properties, we will use the CNDs term in the following text. From natural products to graphite, multiple carbon sources were used for CND's top-down [7] or bottom-up [8] fabrication. Despite the enormous volume of published information, the atomic structure of most parts of CNDs obtained in the fabrication processes remains unsolved unambiguously and lacks proper precision. Note that even after separation and purification procedures, the final products of the CNDs fabrication are often some mixes of different carbon-based compounds/co-products where CNDs are the only prevalent component among many possible other components. Typically, several characterization methods are used to reveal the atomic structure and nanomorphology of the compounds under discussion.

The most popular technique used in studying the unknown structure of nanocarbons is the X-ray diffraction (XRD) method. Powder XRD patterns usually show the presence of a very broad peak in the range of 2Ɵ angles between 18 and 28° degrees for Cu Kα radiation [9,10]. Based on these data, it means that some ordered layered nanocarbons with atomic structures close to graphite among fabricated mixes are present. The shift of the 2Ɵ ≈ 26° XRD peak towards smaller 2Ɵ angles is usually discussed in connection with the increase in the interlayer distance in a stack of nanographene layers compared to the average value of 0.335 nm. In turn, the width of this peak in the XRD pattern characterizes the average particle size in the direction perpendicular to the layers, i.e., stack thickness.

The second powerful technique used to characterize CND is Raman scattering spectroscopy. Usually, Raman spectra of CNDs show two broad superimposed D (∼1345 cm−1) and G (∼1585–1600 cm−1) bands similar to those observed in defective nano-graphite (with turbostratic structure) and few-layered graphene with a valuable number of interior and edge defects [11,12]. Thus, based on these two sources, we can conclude that there is a detectable amount of carbon-based nanostructures constructed from graphene-like layers among all the co-products of the CND synthesis. Note that intensities of D and G peaks measured for different CQDs can be different. For example, the work of S.A. Mathew et al. [13] reported that the intensity of the D peak is about 20% larger than the G peak. On the contrary, the work of Y. Li et al. [14] shows Raman spectra with a G peak about four times larger than the D peak. The width of these pecs is also different in the mentioned works. However, besides highly ordered systems such as graphene and graphite [15], Raman spectra provide only qualitative information about layered carbon structures. Raman spectra measured for activated carbon, reduced graphene oxide [16,17], fullerenes and carbon nanodots [18], carbon deposit [19], and carbon nanocones [18] sow the same broad peaks with different intensities. Even the presence of valuable (10–15%) ordered sp
2 -hybridized carbon among diamonds or amorphous phases can provide similar D and G peaks of various intensities in Raman spectra [20]. A detailed discussion of Raman spectroscopy's limitations in layered carbon systems can be found in the review by A. Merlen et al. [17].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is another indirect method of nano-carbon characterization commonly used to explore CNDs of different types is X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Usually, the specific shape and position of the C1s XPS peak provide valuable information about hybridization and the local environment of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen (if any) atoms in CNDs. The unique shapes of C1s XPS peaks reported in multiple works demonstrate a significant diversity of carbon atoms' local configurations in many synthesized products. Also, many published works show that the XPS spectra of CNDs under study are almost identical to the XPS spectra of graphite and graphene. In these spectra, tiny contributions from non-sp
2 hybridized carbon atoms were detected (see, for example, Refs. [21–24]). In other numerous works, XPS spectra demonstrate the prevalence of carbon atoms with sp
3 hybridization (see, for example, Refs. [25,26]), and another one largest group of published papers demonstrates the presence of similar amounts of carbon atoms both in sp
2 and sp
3 hybridizations [27]. The ratio of sp
2 -to-sp
3 hybridized atoms estimated from XPS spectra varies from 10:1 [23,28] to 2:1 [29,30]. Note that XRD studies provide information describing only ordered crystalline structures found in the nanocarbon co-product mix. On the contrary, XPS and Raman studies offer information about all types of carbon atoms in the mix, either from ordered crystalline carbon structures or disordered (molecules, amorphous) parts of the mix.

In addition to the above-described various indirect characterization techniques (XRD, XPS, Raman), the fourth technique provides some direct knowledge about at least some regular species of studied CND particles. This technique is high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). In almost all published papers where HRTEM studies of CNDs were reported, we can find well-featured images of some round-shaped nanosized objects. Note that these nanoparticles are the only objects with TEM regular crystal patterns directly observed in CNDs containing nanocarbon mixes. Usually, these TEM images show some ordered lattice fringes patterns appearing in the central parts of these nanoparticles. Some examples of these lattice fringes patterns are shown in Fig. 1 a and b. Note that the usual thickness of the CND nanoparticles where such lattice fringes patterns have been observed is about 1.2 nm (see Fig. 1c). In some minor amount of published works, the lattice fringes patterns correspond with those observed in graphite (0.24 and 0.31 nm) and are related to [100] and [001] crystallographic directions in graphite (see, for example, Ref. [33]). Also, some other works reported the presence of lattice fringes patterns with a period of 0.27 nm [34] or other values [35]. However, many works that can be found in the literature report the observation of lattice fringes patterns with a period of 0.21 nm [24,30–32].

Because these TEM patterns are observed in CND products fabricated from different carbon-containing sources and by various synthesis methods, we can exclude the possibility of association of these TEM patterns with oxides or carbide-based nanoparticles, which can be synthesized during the course of CNDs' fabrication process from unavoidable metal impurities. The most straightforward and carbon-based system, which demonstrates close lattice fringes patterns, is a diamond's (111) surface. The sp 3 hybridization of some carbon atoms revealed in XPS data can be considered diamond-related. Nevertheless, neither XRD patterns nor Raman spectra do not demonstrate the presence of any signatures of diamonds. Another possible interpretation is 2D-diamond (diamene) [36,37]. We rule out this interpretation based on the absence of typical diamond-related features in Raman spectra and special conditions, such as high pressure, corresponding with the fabrication of diamene [36]. In addition, both diamonds and diamene have carbon atoms with only sp 3 hybridization, which is in controversy with an experimentally observed combination of HRTEM images of CND having 0.21 nm lattice fringes and a significant prevalence of carbon atoms in sp 2 hybridization over carbon atoms with sp
3 hybridization (see discussion in the previous paragraph). The association of these HRTEM images with another carbon allotrope named Lonsdaleite was ruled out due to the absence of evidence of the existence of this material on the nanoscale [38].

The observed lattice fringes with a period of 0.21 nm can also be associated with the [110] direction of graphite (see Fig. 1d). However, such a pattern can be observed only in the case of AA-stacking in graphite, which is extremely rare and was experimentally observed only in some specially treated samples of graphite [39]. In few-layered graphenes, the HRTEM images of turbostratically disordered structures are usually observed when one graphene layer is slightly rotated (<±15°) relative to another neighboring layer and so on (so-called random azimuthal disorder). HRTEM images of turbostratic graphenes demonstrate various Moire patterns or even unregular dot-like patterns [40]. Since AFM studies report the usual thickness of CND nanoparticles is about 1.2–1.5 nm (see Fig. 1c), corresponding to 4–5 graphene layers, hence azimuthally disordered AA-stacking formation in 4–5 layered nano-graphenes is improbable due to the absence of observed Moire patterns. Note that the intercalating of nano-graphene, as discussed experimentally [41] and theoretically [42], can eliminate Moire patterns but simultaneously eliminate graphite-like features in XRD patterns. Additionally, the relationships between the discussed TEM lattice fringes patterns and the ratio of carbon atoms in sp
2 and sp
3 hybridization states are unclear. Thus, we claim that resolving the nature of these 0.21 nm lattice fringes patterns is an essential step to revealing the atomic structure of the nano-objects observed using HRTEM.

In the theoretical simulations of CNDs, the problem of the formation of these 0.21 nm lattice fringes patterns was ignored entirely (see for review Ref. [42,43]). Standard modeling was usually focused on suggestions and modeling the sources of CNDs' optical activity (light-emitting species). The standard modeling of CNDs included calculations of the electronic structure of some nano-graphenes with or without optically active centers on the edges [44,45] or some aromatic molecules consisting of a few benzene rings that could be synthesized during the process of fabrication (see, for example, [30]), which merged (or not) with results of Raman and XPS studies. Note that the presence of some layered graphitic structures with smaller than in bulk graphite interlayer distance revealed by X-ray diffractometry was ignored in a significant number of simulations of CNDs.

In this work, we report the simulation results of CND with lattice fringes patterns of 0.21 nm period constructed from AA-stacked graphene sheets with some covalent C–C interlayer bonds. We also propose some models for synthesizing this nanocarbon system (CNDs) and check the stability of modeled nanocarbon systems in different environments.

Theoretical modeling was carried out using SIESTA pseudopotential code [46] employing the generalized gradient approximation (GGA-PBE) [47] for the exchange-correlation potential in a spin-polarized mode, and van der Waals correction was also taken into account [48]. A full optimization of the atomic positions was carried out, during which the electronic ground state was consistently found using norm-conserving pseudopotentials [49] for the cores with a double-ξ-plus polarization basis for non-hydrogen atoms and a double-ξ for hydrogen atoms. The forces and total energies were optimized with an accuracy of 0.04 eV Å−1 and 1.0 meV/cell, respectively. The simulations were conducted with a 6 × 6 × 2 Monkhorst-Pack k-point grid for the Brillouin zone sampling [50]. The smearing by Gaussian with 0.05 eV half-width on half-height was used for the densities of the states plot.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Some basic principles of graphene chemistry
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the review of experimental data in the Introduction section, we can conclude that carbon nanoparticles observed in HRTEM studies are built from a few AA-stacked graphene sheets with some interlayer covalent bonds between the layers. The proposed structure is in qualitative agreement with the results of XRD, XPS, and Raman studies. Before discussing possible models for these structures, the peculiarities of graphene chemistry should be briefly discussed. The first peculiarity is the formation of a single out-of-plane covalent bond that provides a breaking double CC in-plane bond with the appearance of the unpaired electron distributed by another sub-lattice (for a more detailed discussion, see Refs. [51,52]). This unpaired electron makes carbon atoms of this sub-lattice very chemically active. Thus, the formation of a single C–C covalent bond between two layers of graphene corresponds with the formation of the second single C–C covalent bond between the same layers or the formation of two new C–C covalent bonds with layers above and below. In the case of the utmost layer, the elimination of unpaired electrons on dangling bonds is realized by the attachment of the functional groups. Since the fabrication of CNDs usually occurs in liquid media and many carbon sources, such as citric acid [53], contain large amounts of hydroxyl groups, we will consider surface passivation only by hydroxyl groups in our simulation. Another peculiarity of graphene chemistry is the corrugation of the graphene sheet after forming an out-of-plane covalent bond. These corrugations propagated in the area with radii of about 1 nm and could significantly affect the distribution pattern of functional groups (see, for example, Ref. [54]). Despite the significant influence of chemical properties, the magnitude of these corrugations is relatively moderate (about 0.01 nm) and does not change the overall patterns of Raman spectra (D and G peaks of various intensities). The key difference in Raman spectra of pristine and oxidized graphene is the intensity of the D peak, which is diminished with the reduction of graphene oxide [55]. Numerous works in this area of CNDs report Raman spectra with distinct but sufficiently broad D peaks related to the presence of point-like and collective (like armchair edges) defects in nano-graphene sheets with sizes less than 2 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Models with a minimal number of interlayer covalent bonds
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on the results of XPS and HRTEM studies, which demonstrate the presence of some layered structures with a thickness of about 1.2 nm and several nanometers, we propose a few layers of graphene with AA-stacking as an initial model for CNDs. At the first step of our modeling, we considered a minimalistic supercell of graphene bilayer with eight carbon atoms in each layer. In this structure, the formation of a single covalent C–C bond between the layers corresponds with the appearance of unpaired electrons on both sides. Hydroxyl groups can be added from both sides of the bilayer to eliminate this radical-like state (see Fig. 2 a). The ratio of carbon atoms with sp
3 and sp
2 hybridization in the simulated structure is 1:4.

The covalent bond formation does not provide visible changes in the in-plane lattice structure of graphene lattice. The value of the in-plane lattice parameter is 0.245 nm, almost identical to pure graphene. Out-of-plane dismissal of carbon atoms is about 0.011 nm, smaller than the magnitude of carbon sheet distortions in graphene oxide [56] and fluorinated [54]. The interlayer distance in the structure shown in Fig. 2a and b is 0.295 nm, close to the interlayer distance in graphite. Thus, the structure shown in Fig. 2b is in qualitative agreement with the results of XRD and XPS studies reported in multiple experiments. The layered structure shown in Fig. 2a and b is similar to layered carbon structures that exhibit D and G peaks, as is the case in experimentally observed CNDs (see the Introduction section). The electronic structure of the system shown in Fig. 2e demonstrates the presence of the bandgap corresponding with the red part of spectra (detailed discussion about the relationship between calculated bandgap and colors of emitted light seen in our recent work. [57]).

The next step of our simulations is the simulation of a similar bilayer with fewer interlayer C–C bonds. For this purpose, we used a supercell containing 16 carbon atoms in each layer. We performed a simulation similar to the one described above for one covalent bond between the layers. The calculations demonstrate that decreasing the number of interlayer covalent bonds per formula unit leads to breaking this bond and decoupling the layers (see Fig. 2c and d) with increasing interlayer distance up to the value closer to graphite (0.326 nm). The electronic structure of the system shown in Fig. 2e demonstrates the vanishing of the bandgap. The peaks near the Fermi level are attributed to dangling bonds in each layer [58].

The next step of our exploration is the simulation of the trilayer graphene from the structure shown in Fig. 1a. For this purpose, we removed the initial model hydroxyl group from the top layer. We also added one more graphene layer with a hydroxyl group on the utmost sides. The location of this group was chosen to passivate a radical-like state created by forming a new interlayer C–C bond. Results of the calculations also demonstrate the decoupling of the layers (see Fig. 1d) with an increase of interlayer distance up to 0.326 nm.

Thus, the structure shown in Fig. 2b describes only a limited number of CNDs. Since the simulations described above correspond with a low number of interlayer C–C bonds, the next reasonable step is increasing the number of these bonds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Model with a larger number of interlayer covalent bonds
ce:section.ce:para:
In the previous section, we considered a structure with one of four carbon atoms having sp
3 hybridization. On the opposite extreme is 2D-diamond (diamene) where all carbon atoms are in sp
3 hybridization. Thus, the reasonable step is considering a structure with an intermediate (1:1) ratio of carbon atoms with sp
3 and sp
2 hybridization. Based on the principles discussed in section 3 and experience of the simulation of CNDs with fewer interlayer C–C bonds, we propose the system shown in Fig. 3 a as the model for CNDs with an equal number of carbon atoms in sp
2 and sp
3 hybridization. The next step in our simulation is increasing the number of layers in the proposed model system. On the contrary to the system shown in Fig. 1, increasing the number of interlayer covalent C–C bonds stabilizes the structure. It makes the formation of the systems built from three and four carbon layers possible, as shown in Fig. 3a–c, and d. The transition from two to four layers leads to a gradual decrease in the bandgap, corresponding to a shift of the absorption edge from the near-UV part of the spectrum to the blue one. An increase in the number of interlayer covalent bonds per unit and, therefore, carbon atoms with sp
3 hybridization leads to a widening of the energy gap (see Fig. 3f vs. Fig. 2e). Further growth of the number of layers provides decoupling between the layers with spontaneous sliding of the layers toward the formation of AB stacking and vanishing of the bandgap (see Fig. S1). Increasing the number of interlayer covalent bonds does not lead to visible changes in in-plane lattice parameters. Therefore, the distance between the neighboring lattice fringes of the TEM crystalline pattern remains close to 0.21 nm. On the contrary, the interlayer distance shrinks to the values of about 0.24–0.25 nm. The overall height of the four-layered structure shown in Fig. 3d is about 1.09 nm, which is close to the profile heights observed in multiple AFM profilometric experiments (approximately 1.2 nm, see Fig. 1c). It should also be borne in mind that the gap between the underlying layer of the CND particle and the substrate, made, for example, of highly oriented pyrolytic carbon, can be about 0.335 nm. Thus, we can conclude that the structure shown in Fig. 3d and e agrees with a whole set of experimental data.

Since published experimental data reports the diversity of the ratios of carbon atoms with sp
2 and sp
3 hybridizations, we simulate the decrease in the number of interlayer covalent bonds by gradually removing some pairs of hydroxyl groups from both sides of the slab. In the case of four-layered structures, even breaking the first pair of C–C bonds in each layer provides the breaking of all interlayer bonds and the decoupling of layers. In the structure with a smaller number of layers, removing the quarter of hydroxyl groups keeps the structure stable (see Fig. 1b). Simulated decreasing the number of carbon atoms in sp
3 hybridization provides shrinking the bandgap (see Fig. 3g). Further removal of hydroxyl groups from the surface of the slab provides decoupling of the layers. The results of these simulations demonstrate the essential role of surface functional groups in forming interlayer covalent bonds.

Another approach for varying the ratio of carbon atoms with different hybridizations is the gradual removal of carbon atoms from internal layers. For this purpose, we checked two other scenarios: formation of bivacancy inside one of the internal planes (Fig. 4 a) and removal of carbon atoms in different layers (see Fig. 4b). In the latter case, the carbon atoms from interlayer C–C bridge were removed. The calculation results demonstrate that different patterns of vacancies correspond with diverse changes in atomic and electronic structure. The formation of bivacancy in one of the internal layers does not provide visible changes in the overall atomic structure of the slab and corresponds with a visible decrease of the energy gap (see Fig. 4c) corresponding with the transition of emission from the estimated blue to estimated green part of the spectra. On the contrary, removing the interlayer carbon bridge provides delamination of the layers and the elimination of the bandgap.

Note that in real CNDs, especially in synthesizing CNDs from natural products, other functional groups can be attached to the surface instead of the hydroxyl group. We simulated the effect of larger functional groups on the surface on the atomic and electronic structure of considered model systems by substituting half hydroxyl to –CH2CH3 groups. The calculated atomic structure demonstrates the tendency to decouple some layers. Still, contrary to complete delamination with the formation of the graphite-like layers (shown in Fig. 4d), in this system, layers remain connected. This connection between the layers distorts the internal graphite flat, decreasing the interlayer distance (0.29 nm). The electronic structure of the system shown in Fig. 4f is also close to the electronic structure of the same model system functionalized with hydroxyl groups (Fig. 3d). To simulate the interaction of the system shown in Fig. 3d with liquid media, we simulated change in atomic structure after attachment of the water molecule to each surface hydroxyl groups. Results of the calculations demonstrate the same patterns of change of atomic and electronic structure as was observed for the –CH2CH3 functionalized system (see Fig. 4e and f). Thus, we can claim that quasi-infinite slabs of two, three, and four graphene layers with functionalized surfaces and multiple interlayer C–C covalent bonds are suitable models for CNDs observed in the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simulation of the formation of AA-stacked graphenic systems
ce:section.ce:para: The last step of our simulations is the discussion of possible fabrication routes of considered systems from sources of carbon used in experiments. In the case of top-down production, the breaking of graphenic sheets occurs in the air or often liquid environments by applying external energies. The formation of dangling bonds on the edges corresponds with oxidation of the edges and near-edge areas [59,60]. The combination of unzipping and oxidation in liquid media leads to the formation of carboxyl groups on the edges. This group can interact with some edge hydrogen or other hydrogen-containing groups with the formation of covalent bridges similar to those shown in Fig. 5 a. Note that the formation of the interlayer bridge is impossible in the case of AB stacking of graphene layers. Another possible route to the building layered structure with AA stacking is the growth of the second layer covalently attached to the graphenic substrate. The work by S. Tachi et al. [61] reported the synthesis of CNDs from graphene oxide and benzyl alcohol. The hydroxyl groups on the surface of graphene oxide can interact with the hydrogen of benzoic acid with the hydroxyl group of graphene oxide following the appearance of a covalent bond between the graphene substrate and aromatic ring, as shown in Fig. 5b and c. One can see the formation of this covalent bridge leads to the location of the aromatic ring over the substrate's hexagonal rings. This covalently attached aromatic ring could be the source of the growth of the second graphenic layer. The multiplicity of hydroxyl groups on the graphene oxide surface is the source of additional covalent bonds with the next layer.

The most common precursor for bottom-up synthesis of CNDs is citric acid. In our recent paper [62], we demonstrate the formation of the seeds of covalently bonded bilayer with AA-stacking of the layers shown in Fig. 5d. In the mentioned paper we also show the step-by-step synthesis of CNDs from citric acid corresponds with the growth of graphenic planes with numerous hydroxyl groups in basal plane (see Fig. 5e). Other sets of reactants also can form the seeds for the growth of graphenic planes with AA-stacking. For example, in a recent paper by J. Xu et al. [30], the synthesis of CNDs from o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and acetic acid has been performed. The possible product of the reactions between these two reactants is shown in Fig. 5f. Two –CH2–COOH groups located in ortho positions on aromatic rings can be the source of the growth of the second aromatic ring by attachment of the second OPD molecule and two –COOH groups will be located in the para position of the central aromatic ring of three-ring structure shown in Fig. 5e. In our previous work [62] we discussed how these two carboxylic group can be the source for interlayer C–C covalent bonds as in structures shown in Figs. 2 and 3 by attachment of the following OPD molecule as it shown in Fig. 5e. On these examples, we demonstrate probable initial stages of the synthesis AA-stacked graphene with interlayer C–C covalent bonds.

In the simulations and discussions presented above, we propose few-layer graphene in AA-stacking with multiple functional groups on the surface and multiple interlayer covalent C–C bonds as the model for larger carbon nanoparticles with 0.21 nm lattice fringes patterns in TEM images observed in numerous experiments. The proposed model is also in good qualitative agreement with the XRD, XPS, and Raman observations reported in the literature. We also considered the possible origin of proposed model structures from the structures formed at the initial stages of the top-down and predominantly bottom-up synthesis of CNDs. Since carbon nanoparticles with 0.21 nm lattice fringes patterns are the only distinct object unambiguously observed among all co-products of the CNDs synthesis, the proposed model should be helpful for the explanation of the main photonic properties of CNDs and further guided synthesis of CNDs.

The calculated electronic structure of the model systems demonstrates that the value of the bandgap depends on the number of layers, number of interlayer bonds, defects, and functionalization. The bandgap widths correspond with the light emission from red to near-UV parts of the spectrum. We cannot definitely claim that the proposed model systems are the source of luminescence in all experiments for two reasons. The first reason is the possibility of the detachment of layers with the breaking of interlayer covalent C–C bonds and the vanishing of the bandgap. Since carbon nanoparticles with 0.21 nm lattice fringe patterns observed in experiments have a large width-to-thickness ratio and can be considered as almost thin flat carbon slabs, the electronic structure of these systems consists of broad density-of-state bands instead of narrow distinct peaks. On the other hand, the luminescence spectra reported in multiple published works also demonstrate rather broad spectral emission peaks. Perhaps the model systems proposed in our work are the source of the luminescence in studied compounds. We can propose two possible scenarios for the simultaneous observation of CNDs with 0.21 nm TEM lattice fringes patterns and luminescence spectra corresponding with quantum dots. The first is the presence of some distinct defect-related density-of-state peaks in the bandgap. Since bottom-up assembling of CNDs or cutting of large carbon-based systems in a top-down process corresponds with the appearance of defects of various types, we can consider this scenario less probable. The second scenario is the formation of some nanosized objects with distinct density-of-state peaks in electronic structure. We can speculate that, in this case, the large carbon nanoparticles discussed in our work can be a radiation harvester with the further transfer of photogenerated electrons (or holes) somehow attached to another nanocarbon species with quantum dots-like fluorescent emission.

Danil W. Boukhvalov: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation. Vladimir Yu. Osipov: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – original draft. Danatbek Murzalinov: Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Abay Serikkanov: Funding acquisition, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Hong Bi: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Data curation, Supervision.

Danatbek Murzalinov reports financial support was provided by Committee of Science of the Ministry of Science and Higher Education of the Republic of Kazakhstan. Abay Serikkanov reports financial support was provided by Committee of Science of the Ministry of Science and Higher Education of the Republic of Kazakhstan. Vladimir Yu. Osipov reports financial support was provided by Physical-Technical Institute named after A F Ioffe Russian Academy of Sciences. Hong Bi reports financial support was provided by National Natural Science Foundation of China. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: D.W.B., D.M., and A.S. acknowledge the support of the Science Committee of the Ministry of Science and Higher Education of the Republic of Kazakhstan (Grant No. BR21881954). The research conducted by V.Yu.O. was carried out within the framework of the Ioffe Institute project FFUG-2024-0019. H. sb:title.sb:maintitle: Longitudinal unzipping of carbon nanotubes to form graphene nanoribbons

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.V. Kosynkin, A.L. Higginbotham, A. Sinitskii, J.R. Lomeda, A. Dimiev, B.K. Price, J.M. Tour, Longitudinal unzipping of carbon nanotubes to form graphene nanoribbons. Nature 458 (2009) 872-876. https://doi.org/10.1038/nature07872

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quan-tum yield enhancement in graphene quantum dots via esterification with benzyl alcohol

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Tachi, H. Morita, M. Takahashi, Y. Okabayashi, T. Hosokai, T. Sugai, S. Kuwahara, Quan-tum Yield Enhancement in Graphene Quantum Dots via Esterification with Benzyl Alcohol. Sci. Rep. 9 (2019) 14115. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-019-50666-3

sb:title.sb:maintitle: First-principles modeling of bottom-up synthesis of carbon quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.W. Boukhvalov, V.Y. Osipov, First-Principles Modeling of Bottom-Up Synthesis of Carbon Quantum Dots. Crystals 13 (2023) 716. https://doi.org/10.3390/cryst13050716
